metrics.narrative: The buyer has articulated operational pain but not quantified financial or cycle-time impact. Johannes Jurack stated on 26 November 2025 that manual processes for supplier collaboration—sharing purchase orders, requests, and certificates—are the biggest pain point, alongside gaps in ESG and risk management that SupplyOn cannot address. He described the current landscape as heavily manual, hindering supplier communication and purchase order visibility. No ROI, cost savings, headcount reduction, or cycle-time improvement has been quantified on any call or in Salesforce fields. The business case for eProcurement was mentioned on 28 January 2026 as under calculation by the purchasing department, but no figures have been shared. Priority-one requirements are supplier collaboration and manual-process elimination; priority-two is ESG and risk management (carbon emissions scope 1-3, adverse media monitoring). The absence of a quantified value case is a known gap; building one is a ranked move.

champion.narrative: Zycus has a developing, partner-mediated champion relationship but no direct internal champion at EFW. The primary channel is Lim Wee Keong (ST Engineering Singapore, partner champion), who orchestrated the initial engagement in October 2025 and has advocated for Zycus by sharing ST Engineering's own RFP evaluation and positioning Zycus as a preferred vendor. The Next Step log (30 October 2025) describes Wee Keong as the 'STE champion,' and he participated in the 26 November 2025 and 28 January 2026 calls, offering to provide RFP insights and support to the relevant stakeholder, Wee Keong is not an EFW employee and cannot drive internal EFW decisions; he is an external advocate. On the EFW side, Johannes Jurack (Speaker for Digitalization and Project Management, S/4H program manager) is engaged and transparent, has attended both buyer calls, and has facilitated the evaluation process, but he has explicitly stated he is 'not so emotional' about the procurement tools and defers purchasing-module decisions to Carina Hieke's manager (head of purchasing, not yet engaged). Johannes has access to the S/4H steering board and management, which is a positive signal, but he has not demonstrated active internal advocacy for Zycus over SAP or other competitors—he is running a neutral market evaluation. Carina Hieke (Supply Chain Manager, or 'Karina' on calls) was intended to join the 28 January 2026 call but was absent due to sick leave; Carl Kimball offered on that call to engage her and her manager directly, and Johannes agreed to brief her, but no follow-up engagement has occurred as of 26 June 2026 (5 months later). No other EFW stakeholder (purchasing head, IT lead, supply chain lead, executive sponsor) has appeared on a call or in Salesforce. The champion relationship is cooling: the last buyer call was 28 January 2026 (5 months ago), the rep's 6 June 2026 email to Johannes received no reply by 26 June (20 days), and the Next Step log records 'No response from project leader. Became silent. KIV' on 30 March 2026. The partner channel remains the only active thread, but the internal EFW champion—who can navigate the steering board, influence the head of purchasing, and advocate for Zycus over SAP—has not been identified or secured. Developing a direct champion relationship with the head of purchasing and a senior S/4H steering-board member is the highest-priority move to re-engage and compete effectively against SAP.

competition.narrative: Zycus is competing in a three-way evaluation against SAP Business Network (formerly Ariba Network, the strategic incumbent given EFW's SAP-first strategy) and SupplyOn (the operational incumbent for supplier collaboration). SAP is the highest current threat. Johannes stated on 26 November 2025 that 'SAP as main competitor' and on 28 January 2026 confirmed that EFW's strategy is 'SAP for first strategy... if we don't get the right solution, then we can also exchange,' meaning SAP is the default and Zycus must displace it on fit and capability. The buyer is evaluating SAP Business Network specifically for supplier collaboration, onboarding, and certificate/code-of-conduct exchange (the same use case Zycus iSupplier targets). SAP's advantage is strategic alignment with the S/4HANA transformation and EFW's enterprise SAP footprint (ECC today, S/4H in 2027); its weakness is functional fit—Johannes asked on 28 January 2026 whether Zycus's supplier-network coverage rate is higher than SAP's, implying SAP's network may be a concern. SupplyOn is the current platform for supplier collaboration but cannot address ESG, risk management, or AI capabilities, creating the capability gap that opened this evaluation. SupplyOn is not a live threat for new modules (it is being evaluated for replacement) but remains the baseline for collaboration workflows. Coupa and iValua are named in Salesforce Competitors__c but have not been mentioned on any call or in Next Step notes; their status is unknown—they may be historical or placeholder entries with no active engagement. The wedge to win against SAP is functional superiority in supplier collaboration (network coverage, ease of onboarding, workflow configurability), AI-driven risk and ESG capabilities (adverse media monitoring, carbon tracking), and eProcurement depth (the purchasing department was 'really interested' in the iManage demo per 28 January call), combined with SAP-certified integration adapters to mitigate the 'SAP-first' bias. The ST Engineering reference (partner-led proof point, shared RFP evaluation, positive workshop feedback) is a secondary wedge. The risk is that resource constraints and the 'SAP-first' strategy lead the buyer to default to the relevant stakeholder as the path of least resistance, even if functionally weaker, unless Zycus builds a strong internal champion and executive-level sponsorship to counter that bias.

identify_pain.narrative: The buyer's pain is operational inefficiency in supplier collaboration and a strategic gap in ESG and risk management. Johannes Jurack (Speaker for Digitalization and Project Management, program manager for S/4HANA transformation) owns the S/4H procurement-modernization stream and is the pain owner for collaboration and ESG gaps. He stated on 26 November 2025 that 'our pain points, this are the many manual processes... the biggest point is the collaboration with the suppliers to share purchase orders, to share the request, yeah, this is our pain point together with this soft things regarding ESG and risk management.' The current landscape is SAP ECC with SupplyOn as the sole procurement platform; SupplyOn cannot address ESG, risk management, or AI-driven capabilities, creating a capability gap. The purchasing department (led by Carina Hieke's manager, head of purchasing) experiences the day-to-day pain of manual PO and certificate exchange, which hinders supplier communication and purchase order visibility. The supply chain team (Carina Hieke, Sarah Haertel) is involved in evaluating supplier collaboration and eProcurement solutions to address these pains. Johannes noted on 26 November 2025 that 'the current lack of AI utilization at EFW is identified as a significant problem,' though legal/compliance constraints in Germany create friction for AI adoption. The pain is urgent enough to drive a market evaluation during a resource-constrained S/4H transformation, but the urgency has not been quantified financially (no cost-of-delay, no cycle-time target, no ROI target stated). The compelling event is the S/4HANA go-live in 2027: the buyer wants to align the S2P platform rollout with the ERP transformation to avoid a second disruptive change post-go-live. Johannes stated on 28 January 2026 that 'we want also move the decision making things later' to synchronize timelines, reflecting both the compelling event and the resource constraint.

The ST Engineering partner has shared RFP collateral and may provide informal guidance, but no formal paper-process timeline or approvals chain has been mapped. The buyer has not reached pricing, so contract negotiation has not started. Resource constraints due to the S/4H transformation may delay or compress the contracting phase. Understanding the InfoSec, legal, and procurement-approval requirements—and their typical cycle time at EFW—is a priority move before the November-December 2026 vendor decision.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process is a two-track, committee-driven evaluation aligned with a multi-year S/4HANA transformation program. Track one: supplier collaboration and risk modules (iRisk, iSupplier network) are governed by the S/4HANA transformation steering board and management committee, with Johannes Jurack as the program-management lead. Decisions on these modules are targeted for mid-2026. Track two: eProcurement (iManage, PR/PO, intake) is owned by the head of purchasing (Carina Hieke's manager, not named) and the purchasing department, independent of the S/4H steering. Johannes stated on 28 January 2026 that 'these things for eProcurement, this is a decision they have to make—the purchasing department, the head of the purchasing department.' The overall vendor decision is scheduled for November-December 2026 per the Next Step log (28 January, 2 February 2026) and the 28 January call, with realization kickoff in late 2026 and S2P go-live deferred to 2027 to align with S/4HANA rollout (targeted 2027). The buyer is running a market evaluation (not yet a formal RFP as of 28 January 2026) comparing Zycus against SAP Business Network, SupplyOn (incumbent), and potentially iValua and Coupa. ST Engineering Singapore (partner, champion Lim Wee Keong) is leading the engagement from Singapore on behalf of EFW Germany and has shared its own RFP evaluation documentation as reference. The process includes multiple module-specific demos, integration deep-dives with IT and supply-chain stakeholders, and a supplier-network coverage-rate analysis. An RFP launch was mentioned for March 2026 in early Next Step entries (December 2025, January 2026) but has not materialized; the timeline shifted when the ERP transformation strategy changed in late 2025-early 2026 (manufacturing-system Phase 1 in 2026, S/4H Phase 2 in 2027). Johannes noted on 28 January 2026 that 'it's quite hard to get the resources for projects because the ERP or the s 4 transformation will spend a lot of resources,' indicating resource contention is a process constraint. No multi-approvalchain or CFO sign-off has been described, and no legal, InfoSec, or procurement-review gate has been discussed. The buyer has not yet moved to pricing, contracting, or reference-customer validation.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are partially documented and prioritized. The buyer has explicitly ranked requirements into two tiers. Priority one: end-to-end supplier collaboration (purchase order issuance, order confirmations, delivery advice, invoice workflows) to eliminate manual PO sharing and certificate exchange, which are described as 'the biggest point' of pain. Priority two: ESG and risk management, including carbon emissions tracking (scope 1, 2, 3), adverse media monitoring via AI, and supply risk scorecarding. Johannes stated on 26 November 2025 that contract management (iContract) is not a priority. Integration with SAP S/4HANA is a confirmed requirement: the buyer is evaluating certified SAP adapters and CPI integration, and Johannes confirmed on 28 January 2026 that they plan to connect modules to the relevant stakeholder now and transition to S/4HANA when ready. The buyer is comparing Zycus iSupplier and supplier-network collaboration against SAP Business Network (formerly Ariba Network) and the incumbent SupplyOn on collaboration and ESG fit. eProcurement (iManage intake, PR/PO, catalog) was demoed on or before 2 December 2025 and positively received by the purchasing department. German-language presentation support was flagged as preferred but not mandatory. Use-case demos and integration workshops were requested for Q4 2025 but have not yet occurred. The RFP timeline remains unclear; Johannes stated on 28 January 2026 that an RFP launch is planned but the project plan may shift based on SAP Digital Manufacturing Cloud implementation. No formal BRD or RFP document has been shared with Zycus.

ai_fit_signal.summary: The buyer is AI Curious with structural openness constrained by German legal/compliance friction. Johannes stated on 26 November 2025 that 'the current lack of AI utilization at EFW is identified as a significant problem' and that 'everyone's wanted, but it's hard from the legal side... it's a problem in Germany.' He confirmed on that call that EFW is 'pro AI now' and that AI scrutiny (non-ChatGPT, functionally-focused AI) is acceptable. The priority-two requirement for ESG and risk management explicitly includes AI-driven adverse media monitoring, signaling genuine appetite. However, AI has not been a top-of-mind topic in subsequent conversations—it did not resurface on the 28 January 2026 call, and no AI-specific use case, demo, or ROI discussion has occurred. The ST Engineering partner reference includes positive AI/Merlin feedback from workshops, but that has not been translated into EFW-specific AI engagement. Overall, the buyer is conceptually open and sees AI as a capability gap vs incumbents (SupplyOn, SAP), but AI appetite is secondary to core collaboration and integration priorities and has not been activated as a competitive wedge.

best_practice_check.summary: This deal requires immediate re-engagement to restart momentum and counter SAP's strategic advantage. Three high-leverage win-strategy moves: (1) Re-establish direct engagement with Johannes and secure executive access to the head of purchasing and S/4H steering-board sponsor—the partner channel alone cannot navigate internal politics or counter the SAP-first bias. (2) Co-author the eProcurement business case and ROI model with the purchasing department to convert their conceptual interest into a quantified, board-ready financial justification that isolates Zycus's value vs SAP's. (3) Deliver the supplier-network coverage analysis and the standalone collaboration demo Johannes requested on 28 January 2026 to address the single largest functional objection (network coverage) and prove Zycus outperforms SAP and SupplyOn on the buyer's priority-one requirement.

competitive_position.summary: SAP Business Network (Ariba Network) is the highest current threat, positioned as the strategic default under EFW's 'SAP-first' strategy and the S/4HANA transformation program. SAP's advantage is enterprise alignment and procurement-team familiarity, but its weakness is functional fit—Johannes's question about supplier-network coverage on 28 January 2026 signals SAP's network may be a gap. SupplyOn is the operational incumbent for supplier collaboration but is being evaluated for replacement due to inability to address ESG, risk, and AI requirements; it is not a live threat for new-module wins but sets the collaboration-workflow baseline. Coupa and iValua are named in Salesforce but have not appeared on calls or Next Step notes since October 2025; their status is dormant or historical. Zycus's wedge is functional superiority in supplier collaboration (network coverage, onboarding ease, workflow depth), AI-driven ESG and risk capabilities (adverse media, carbon tracking), eProcurement strength (positively received by purchasing), and SAP-certified integration—combined with the ST Engineering reference as proof. The risk is that resource constraints, the S/4H transformation workload, and the 'SAP-first' bias lead the buyer to default to SAP as the path of least resistance unless Zycus secures a strong internal champion and executive sponsorship to counter that strategic inertia.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: EFW's expectations center on eliminating manual supplier-collaboration workflows, adding AI-driven ESG and risk management, and integrating seamlessly with their SAP ECC-to-S/4HANA transformation. Zycus is well aligned on collaboration workflow automation (iSupplier network positively received conceptually), ESG/risk/AI capabilities (carbon tracking, adverse media monitoring demonstrated on 26 November call), and SAP integration (certified adapters noted). However, two exposures exist: (1) supplier-network coverage rate is unconfirmed—Johannes asked on 28 January whether Zycus's network matches EFW's existing supplier base, and coverage was estimated at ~10 percent pending precise calculation, which may fall short of SAP's or SupplyOn's existing footprint; (2) eProcurement business case and ROI are under buyer calculation, but Zycus has not yet co-authored that case or quantified the value, leaving the purchasing department's interest unsupported by financial justification. German-language support was flagged as preferred but not mandatory and can be met via local Zycus Germany team. Overall fit is strong on functionality and strategy but partially exposed on network coverage and value quantification.

deltas.subject: Re-engage Johannes directly and escalate to executive engagement with head of purchasing and S/4H steering-board sponsor to counter SAP-first bias and restart internal advocacy. The partner channel (ST Engineering) is valuable but insufficient—SAP will win by default unless Zycus builds direct, multi-threaded relationships with the purchasing leadership and steering-board executives who control the vendor decision. Carl offered on 28 January to meet the head of purchasing; Johannes agreed to facilitate but no follow-up occurred. Rep email 6 June received no reply by 26 June (20 days). The deal is cooling. Priority move: Carl or the deal owner's manager (executive connect) should reach Johannes and the purchasing head directly within 7 days to re-anchor the evaluation, offer German executive support, and secure a near-term call with the steering-board sponsor.

deltas.to: Co-author the eProcurement business case and ROI model with the purchasing department to convert conceptual interest into a board-ready financial justification.
deltas.subject: Co-author the eProcurement business case and ROI model with the purchasing department to convert conceptual interest into a board-ready financial justification. Johannes stated on 28 January that the purchasing department is 'really interested' in eProcurement but must calculate the business case. Zycus has not yet partnered on that calculation, leaving the buyer to quantify value alone—a gap SAP will exploit by offering bundled S/4H-Business Network pricing and ease-of-procurement narratives. Activate a value-engineering or ROI-workshop engagement (Zycus team + purchasing + finance) to model manual-process elimination savings (PO cycle time, supplier onboarding cost, collaboration FTE reduction) and ESG/risk-management ROI (adverse-media cost avoidance, carbon-reporting compliance cost). The output is a co-authored, peer-validated business case that isolates Zycus's incremental value over SAP and becomes the purchasing head's ammunition to justify a non-SAP choice to the steering board.

deltas.to: Deliver the supplier-network coverage analysis and standalone iSupplier collaboration demo to close the single largest functional-fit objection and prove Zycus
deltas.subject: Deliver the supplier-network coverage analysis and standalone iSupplier collaboration demo to close the single largest functional-fit objection and prove Zycus outperforms SAP and SupplyOn on priority-one requirements. Johannes explicitly requested both on 28 January 2026; neither has been delivered by 26 June (5 months later). Network coverage is a decision criterion—if Zycus's coverage is materially lower than SAP's or SupplyOn's, it becomes a disqualifier; if equal or higher, it neutralizes SAP's bundled-network advantage. The demo must show end-to-end collaboration workflow (PO issuance, confirmation, delivery advice, invoice submission, certificate exchange) with live examples of configurability, ease of supplier onboarding, and AI-driven risk/ESG overlays that SAP and SupplyOn cannot match. Deliver both within 14 days of re-engagement to reset momentum and give Johannes tangible proof points to advocate internally.
